01 — Who I Am

The PM Lens, Built from the
Support Trenches

Five years ago I started in support. What I didn't realise then — and understand completely now — is that the support function is the single highest-density source of product intelligence in any SaaS company. Every ticket is a user telling you, in unfiltered terms, exactly where your product breaks down.

At Maropost I identified a recurring failure pattern across 80+ support tickets, built the case for a native fix, led the discovery process, and coordinated cross-functionally to ship JetSend in under 6 weeks. At HighLevel I escalated structured product gaps monthly to Engineering and configured AI agents for SMB clients and internal teams. At Vartopia I was the customer's voice in sprint prioritisation and identified the CRM form-value passthrough gap.

That is not support work. That is product work done without the title. I am now seeking to own the PM side of this equation formally.

Customer Support Representative → HighLevel
Apr 2025 – Feb 2026
Remote · US market · B2B SaaS CRM platform · 2M+ businesses · White-label
  • 30% drop in inbound ticket volume and 20% CSAT improvement across SMB white-label accounts, because clients needed faster first-response on repeat queries, by configuring and validating AI-powered chatbot automation workflows including conversational flows and webhook-triggered logic.
  • AHT reduced 37%, from 40 to 25 minutes, and CSAT up 5% within 8 weeks, because the support team was losing hours to manual knowledge lookup, by defining requirements, writing functional documentation, and coordinating the build and adoption of an internal AI agent.
  • 2 workflow automation improvements shipped in subsequent sprints, because recurring product gaps were going unaddressed, by synthesising 80+ monthly tickets into structured gap reports with user stories and acceptance criteria for the product team.
  • Faster post-release readiness across support and customer success, because teams lacked clarity on what changed with each release, by preparing internal release notes, product documentation, FAQs, and enablement materials ahead of every cycle.
Technical Support Engineer → Vartopia

On-site · Punjab, India · Enterprise SaaS · Salesforce partner management

  • Reduced false escalations and improved engineering trust, because misdiagnosed issues were wasting engineering time, by building a pre-escalation validation checklist and applying structured QA and UAT processes before raising any issue.
  • Sprint backlog input accepted from a support role, because a manual data re-entry error pattern had no product fix, by identifying and proposing automation of form-value passthrough between CRM systems.

  • Data integrity issues surfaced and resolved faster, because conversations were running on intuition rather than evidence, by using SQL queries to validate anomalies and bring concrete proof to engineering discussions.

03 — Product Initiatives

Real Product Work,
Evidence-First

JetSend — Transactional Messaging
Maropost · CRM & Marketing Automation · 2024
Shipped & Live
My RoleDiscovery Initiator · Requirements Contributor · Cross-functional Coordinator
Teams InvolvedProduct · Engineering · QA · Customer Success
TimelineZero to GA in under 6 weeks
Hypothesis
Enterprise clients were abandoning transactional workflows not because of user error, but because the platform had no native reliability layer — forcing dangerous workarounds that silently failed at scale.
How I Found It — Before Any PM Was Involved
  • Noticed a recurring ticket pattern: 3–5 enterprise clients per week reporting failed transactional sends — each escalated as a one-off, never surfaced as systemic
  • Ran my own analysis across ticket history. Found the same three failure modes every time: no delivery status visibility, no retry logic, no failure alerting — product gaps, not config errors
  • Client behaviour evidence: multiple accounts had built manual workarounds (spreadsheet-tracked resend queues) — the problem was critical enough to absorb significant operational cost
  • I brought this to the PM team — it was not a task I was given. I initiated the conversation with the evidence already structured
Discovery Process I Ran With the PM
  • Conducted structured client interviews alongside the PM — gathering pain points, workaround behaviour, and willingness-to-switch signals
  • Created a detailed JTBD document with supporting references — this became the requirements foundation
  • Enterprise marketing ops teams needed guaranteed delivery with audit trails for compliance — platform offered neither
Trade-offs Considered (MECE)
  • Option A — Documentation fix only: Rejected. The failure was architectural, not instructional.
  • Option B — Per-client workaround support: Rejected. Does not scale, creates tech debt.
  • Option C — Native transactional layer (JetSend) ✓ Selected: Addresses root cause, scales, closes the competitive gap.
<6w
Zero to GA
3
Capability pillars shipped
80+
Tickets analysed
Self-init
Discovery initiated without PM prompt
Honest Reflection
I did not own a formal post-launch metrics review. I know ticket volume dropped in the targeted categories from qualitative signal — but I did not establish a rigorous before/after baseline. In future work, I will define success metrics upfront before launch, not after.
Pattern RecognitionRequirements DefinitionCross-functional Coord.DiscoveryJTBD
JetSend Live
AI Support Agents — SMB & Internal
HighLevel · B2B SaaS CRM · White-label · 2025
Shipped & Measured
My RoleConfiguration Lead · Product Input · Outcomes Tracker
ScopeTwo agents: external (SMB clients) + internal (support team)
Timeline6-week deployment cycle each
Problem
HighLevel's white-label clients (SMBs) were burning support hours on repeat, low-complexity queries. Internally, agents were spending 40+ minutes per ticket navigating fragmented playbooks. Both problems had the same fix: AI-augmented routing and response.
SMB Client AI Agent (External)
  • Configured the AI agent within HighLevel's ecosystem for white-label client deployments — mapping the most frequent ticket categories to structured resolution flows
  • Worked with clients to define intent categories, escalation thresholds, and fallback paths — ensuring the agent handled routine queries without human intervention
  • Monitored deflection rates post-deployment and flagged gaps in coverage back to the product team as structured improvement inputs
  • Result: 30% reduction in ticket volume communicated by clients, 20% CSAT improvement within 6 weeks
Internal Support Team AI Agent
  • Leveraged structured chat data from the internal support system to configure an agent that surfaced the right playbook step, article, or escalation path within the agent's workflow
  • Reduced average handling time (AHT) from 40 to 25 minutes — a 37.5% reduction — and contributed to a 5% CSAT improvement
  • Documented the configuration logic and edge-case handling as internal knowledge base articles — ensuring continuity beyond my tenure
30%
Ticket volume drop (SMB)
20%
CSAT increase (SMB)
37.5%
AHT reduction (internal)
+5%
CSAT (internal)
Honest Reflection
I was the configurator and tracker, not the model trainer. The AI infrastructure was HighLevel's platform. My contribution was the structured product thinking: intent mapping, escalation design, and outcome measurement. That distinction matters and I won't inflate it.

HighLevel CRM — Product Teardown
Independent Product Thinking · 2026
Product Thinking
Format9-section editorial HTML teardown
Basis10 months of daily enterprise-level use
StatusPublished live
What I Did
After 10 months embedded in HighLevel's ecosystem daily, I structured my observations into a 9-section product teardown covering: core value prop, user segmentation, UX friction points, automation builder limitations, AI capabilities, competitive gaps, and three specific prioritised improvements I would ship as PM.
Key Findings From the Teardown
  • Automation builder: Works well for linear flows. Breaks down at conditional branching with 2+ outcome paths — no native error-state visibility at the branch level. Failures surface through client complaints, not platform signals.
  • White-label positioning: A genuine moat — but the agency onboarding flow assumes too much technical literacy from non-technical white-label clients, creating early churn risk
  • AI features: Promising but siloed. The AI agent, conversation AI, and workflow AI don't share context — leading to redundant configuration effort for clients who want end-to-end automation
  • Three changes I'd prioritise: (1) branch-level error visibility in the automation builder, (2) a unified AI context layer across the platform's AI features, (3) a simplified non-technical onboarding track for white-label client sub-accounts
9
Sections documented
10mo
Inside the platform

PM recommendations

Read Full Teardown
Maropost Marketing Cloud — Product Teardown
Independent Product Thinking · 11 months inside the platform · 2024
Product Thinking
Format9-section editorial HTML teardown · B2B SaaS · Email + Ecommerce Automation
Basis11 months as Client Support Specialist · cross-referenced with G2 Winter 2025 reports
StatusPublished live · shiwal50.github.io/portfolio/maropost-teardown.html
What I Did
After 11 months embedded in Maropost daily — supporting enterprise clients like Mercedes-Benz Canada, BioTrust Nutrition, and REDEF Group — I structured my product observations into a 9-section teardown covering architecture, user segments, journey friction, feature depth, competitive positioning, and three prioritised PM recommendations.
Key Findings From the Teardown
  • eRFM AI scoring is the real moat: No other platform at this price tier combines Recency, Frequency, Monetary, and Email engagement data into automatic audience scoring without manual tagging — this is a genuine differentiator that Klaviyo charges significantly more for
  • Onboarding is the biggest churn risk: The platform's power is real — but the path to that power requires upfront technical configuration (custom field mapping, domain auth, journey logic) that non-technical mid-market teams consistently stumble on. Support tickets from this friction dominated my queue.
  • Agency use case is underbuilt: Agencies managing multiple brand accounts are using the platform for multi-client management but there is no unified agency dashboard — they're working around this with individual logins, which creates unnecessary operational overhead and churn risk
  • Three changes I'd prioritise as PM: (1) smart field-matching on CSV import to reduce onboarding friction, (2) a formal agency dashboard with cross-account reporting, (3) transactional messaging natively within the platform — which is exactly the gap I later addressed with JetSend
Why This Matters for My PM Story
  • The JetSend initiative I led at Maropost was a direct output of this analytical mindset — I was pattern-matching across client pain points and surfacing them as structured product inputs before I ever wrote a formal teardown
  • This teardown is the documented version of how I think: from user behaviour evidence to hypothesis to prioritised recommendation — the same loop I apply to every product problem

RouCN — D2C Sneaker Brand · India
Independent PM Case Study · Founder · GTM Lead · 2025–Ongoing
Live PM Case Study — Pre-Launch
ⓘ This is an independent PM case study built to demonstrate discovery-to-roadmap thinking across 7 documented sections. No inventory produced. No manufacturer finalised. All research, personas, and roadmap are the PM deliverable, not operational outputs.
Budget (planned)₹10,00,000
Launch TargetAugust 2026 · D2C first
Case Study Status7 sections documented
Hypothesis
Indian Gen Z sneaker buyers in the ₹2,500–4,500 price band are underserved not on price, but on identity. The first Indian D2C brand to crack narrative + quality in this band will earn disproportionate loyalty. RouCN's raccoon positioning — scrappy, adaptive, nocturnal, everywhere — is ownable at this price point.
Market Signal
  • India footwear market: $20.67B in 2025, growing at 9.7–12.9% CAGR through 2033
  • Competitive gap: Nike/Adidas enter at ₹7,000+; local brands compete on price with no identity — no brand owns the mid-market with a story
  • BIS QCO 2024 compliance becomes a structural moat against informal sellers who cannot list on regulated marketplaces
12-Month Planned Roadmap
Phase 1 · May–Jul 2026
Foundation
  • Manufacturer selection · BIS cert
  • Brand identity: raccoon, 3 colourways
  • Shopify storefront · 150-pair first run
Phase 2 · Aug 2026
D2C Launch
  • Instagram Reels campaign · Drop launch
  • Target: 50 orders in first 30 days
  • CAC target < ₹800
Phase 3 · Sep–Oct 2026
Marketplace Expansion
  • Myntra listing · Festive campaign
  • Meta ads scaled on D2C ROAS learnings
Phase 4 · Nov 2026–Apr 2027
Scale & Review
  • Data review · SKU 2 decision gate
  • Amazon · Pop-up exploration
7
Research sections documented
4

4

04 — McKinsey Forward

Structured Thinking,
Certified

McKinsey.org
Forward Program Graduate

The McKinsey.org Forward program is a competitive online learning program that equips participants with practical skills for the future of work. It covers the McKinsey approach to problem-solving, effective communication frameworks, and foundational digital tools — the same structured thinking that McKinsey applies to real client engagements.

I completed this program concurrently with my PM transition, using it specifically to sharpen the structured thinking skills that matter most in product: MECE problem decomposition, the Pyramid Principle for stakeholder communication, and adaptive mindsets for ambiguous product environments.

Certified · Jun 23, 2026 · McKinsey.org
Issued · Jun 23, 2026
McKinsey.org Forward Program
Problem SolvingMcKinsey's structured approach to decomposing complex problems — MECE thinking applied to product decisions
Pyramid PrincipleHypothesis-first communication — how I write PRDs, escalation summaries, and stakeholder updates
Effective CommunicationStructuring insights to be decision-ready, not just informative — a skill I use daily in product gap reporting
Digital FoundationsFoundational digital toolkit for the future of work — complementing my hands-on SaaS and AI product experience

McKinsey Forward Program

Product Management with AI

A rigorous program delivered by Physics Wallah in partnership with NSDC (National Skill Development Corporation) and PwC, covering the full intersection of modern product management and artificial intelligence. The program addressed AI-driven product discovery, building and validating AI-powered features, integrating LLMs into product workflows, and using AI tools to accelerate PRD writing, user research synthesis, and roadmap prioritisation. 08 — Honest Gaps

What I Know I'm Still
Working On

The best PMs I've studied are honest about what they don't know yet. Here's mine — unfiltered.

01
Structured User Research
My discovery has relied primarily on ticket pattern analysis and internal inference. Structured interview sprints are a deliberate focus area — planned to begin Q3 2026 for RouCN.
02
Public Product Writing
The thinking exists. LinkedIn article series begins Q3 2026, one post per week. The HighLevel teardown is the first published artefact.
03
Post-launch Metrics Rigour
JetSend showed me the gap: I defined success metrics after launch, not before. Every future initiative will have a pre-agreed, time-boxed measurement plan from day one.

Three unsolicited LinkedIn recommendations from people I worked with directly. All public and verifiable.

He took it upon himself to talk to stakeholders, understand what clients actually needed from transactional messaging, and turned that into structured requirements the product team could use.

That kind of initiative is rare in a support role. Two enterprise clients actually brought up the JetSend improvements during their renewal conversations which felt like a direct reflection of the groundwork he'd put in.

He'd be a strong addition to any product team.

Maropost
Senior to Shiwal · Jun 2026
Shiwal was different — he'd notice patterns in what was coming in, flag recurring issues before they became a bigger problem, and actually think about how to reduce the noise rather than just work through it.

He'd document findings properly, build out response templates for common issues, and put together resolution guides that the whole team ended up using. Our average handling time came down. Stale tickets stopped piling up.

You'd point him in a direction and he'd come back having already thought three steps ahead.

Direct Manager · Apr 2026
Most people in support are focused on closing the ticket in front of them. Shiwal was always thinking one step ahead — why is this coming up again, what's actually broken, and who needs to know about it.

He didn't just flag things and disappear. He'd follow through and make sure engineering actually had what they needed, check back on the fix, and then go back to the customer himself. That's not common.

He's switching to product management now and it genuinely doesn't surprise me.
